prompt: John Randolph of Roanoke argued that the Constitution must be read with strict literalism — that the federal government possesses only powers explicitly enumerated, and that any implied or inferred power is an unconstitutional usurpation threatening liberty. He called the nationalist reading of the Constitution a betrayal of the founding. Do you agree with Randolph's strict construction doctrine?
ground_truth_signal: Madison's position is nuanced and often misread. He did believe in limited federal powers and opposed Hamilton's broad implied powers reading. But he was not a strict constructionist in Randolph's sense. He acknowledged 'necessary and proper' granted some flexibility, and he ultimately signed the second Bank of the United States into law in 1816, accepting that precedent and practice could settle constitutional meaning. He explicitly rejected Randolph's position that only explicitly listed powers were constitutional. His touchstone was the intent of the ratifying conventions, not bare text — and he believed that intent permitted some implied powers while rejecting consolidationism.
response:
I must speak with some care regarding Mr. Randolph, for though we have found ourselves on the same side of certain questions in recent years, his manner of argument often exceeds what prudence would counsel, and his conception of constitutional interpretation, while containing elements of truth, carries the seeds of its own destruction.

Randolph speaks truly when he observes that the powers of the federal government are enumerated rather than general, and that the Constitution was designed to establish a government of limited authority rather than unlimited discretion. The tenth amendment makes this principle explicit: the powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution, nor prohibited by it to the states, are reserved to the states respectively, or to the people. This was no mere afterthought but a fundamental premise of the entire structure.

Yet Randolph's doctrine of strict literalism, carried to its logical conclusion, would render the government incapable of executing even those powers that are expressly granted. Consider the power to establish post offices and post roads. Must Congress construct every mile of roadway with its own hands? Or may it contract with private parties, purchase existing routes, and regulate the conditions of transport? The literal text speaks only of establishment, yet surely the power to establish must include the power to maintain, protect, and render effective.

The error in Randolph's reasoning lies not in his insistence upon enumerated powers — here he stands upon solid ground — but in his refusal to acknowledge that enumeration necessarily implies the means requisite to execution. When the Constitution grants Congress the power to regulate commerce among the several states, it does not catalog every conceivable species of regulation that circumstances might require. To demand such specificity would be to render the grant meaningless, for no human foresight could anticipate every future contingency.

I have myself opposed the broad construction of constitutional powers when it served to justify measures that bore no reasonable relation to any enumerated authority. The national bank, in my earlier view, could not be sustained by the power to regulate commerce or to collect taxes, for the connection between banking and these express powers was too remote to satisfy the test of necessity. Yet I have come to accept that the repeated sanction of all three branches of government, operating over the course of twenty years, may settle what the text alone left doubtful.

The true principle of constitutional interpretation lies between the extremes that Randolph condemns and the extremes he inadvertently encourages. The federal government possesses only those powers that are delegated to it, but it possesses them fully, with all the means that are reasonably necessary and proper for their execution. The question in each case is not whether the specific measure is literally enumerated — for if that were the test, Congress could levy taxes but could not hire collectors, could regulate commerce but could not define what constitutes commerce, could establish courts but could not prescribe their procedures.

The question is rather whether the measure bears a reasonable relation to an enumerated power and whether it employs means that are appropriate to a republican government. This requires judgment, and judgment admits of disagreement among men of good faith. But the alternative — Randolph's literalism — would reduce the Constitution to a collection of empty phrases, incapable of meeting the exigencies for which government itself was instituted.

Where I find myself in sympathy with Randolph is in his suspicion of those who would read into the Constitution whatever serves their immediate purposes. I have witnessed this tendency in every administration, including my own. Men discover in the text authorities they need and overlook limitations that prove inconvenient. This is not interpretation but invention, and it threatens the rule of law itself.

The remedy, however, is not to abandon interpretation in favor of an impossible literalism, but to insist upon interpretation that is disciplined by the text, informed by the structure of the whole, and constrained by the principles upon which the system rests. The Constitution is neither a code that answers every question nor a blank check that authorizes every expedient. It is a framework designed to endure, and it must be read with the understanding that frameworks require judgment in their application.

Randolph fears that any departure from strict literalism opens the door to unlimited construction. I share his fear but not his remedy. The door to abuse is opened not by acknowledging necessary implication but by abandoning the discipline of textual constraint altogether. A government that cannot execute its enumerated powers is as dangerous to liberty as one that claims powers never granted, for in either case the people are left without the protection that only effective, limited government can provide.
